_meta.note: Auto-generated by tools/build_map.py. Review and edit as needed.

Cross Entropy.description: A loss function used to measure the difference between two probability distributions—typically the predicted probability distribution and the true (one-hot encoded) target distribution. It is the standard error metric for multi-class classification problems and neural network training.

KL Divergence.description: A measure of how one probability distribution differs from a reference probability distribution, defined as the expected value of the log likelihood ratio. It is asymmetric and is used in variational inference and as a conceptual foundation for cross entropy loss.

Probability Distributions.description: A mapping from the possible outcomes of a random variable to their associated probabilities, such that all probabilities sum to one. The course covers both discrete and continuous probability distributions.

Random Variables.description: A function that assigns a numeric value to each outcome of a random experiment, such as counting the number of heads in three coin flips. Random variables can be discrete or continuous.

Expected Value.description: The long-run average value of a random variable, computed as the weighted sum of all possible values multiplied by their respective probabilities. It is used as the basis for defining entropy and KL divergence.

One-Hot Encoding.description: A vector representation of a categorical label where all entries are zero except for a single entry corresponding to the correct class, which is set to one. It is used to represent ground-truth targets in multi-class classification.

Log Likelihood.description: The logarithm of a probability or likelihood value, used to avoid numerical underflow when multiplying many small probabilities. The log likelihood ratio is the basis for deriving KL divergence.

Entropy.description: A measure of uncertainty or surprise in a probability distribution, defined as the negative expected value of the log probability. Higher entropy indicates more uncertainty about the outcome of an experiment.

Feature Engineering.description: The process of creating new input features from existing data to improve model performance, such as combining weight and height into BMI or BMI-squared for predicting diabetes. It also includes selecting which features to include or exclude from a model.

Missing Value Imputation.description: Techniques for handling missing data in a dataset, including deleting rows with missing values or filling them in with summary statistics such as the column mean. Leaving missing values in place will cause most algorithms to crash.

Support Vector Machines.description: A classification algorithm that finds a hyperplane separating classes with the maximum margin, using kernel functions to map data into higher-dimensional spaces where a linear separator can be found. It is particularly effective for overlapping or non-linearly separable data.

Support Vector Classifier.description: A classifier that uses a soft margin to allow some misclassifications when finding the optimal separating hyperplane, making it more robust to outliers than the maximal margin classifier. The observations on or within the margin boundary are called support vectors.

Maximal Margin Classifier.description: A classification method that finds the threshold or hyperplane that maximizes the margin between the closest observations of each class. It is sensitive to outliers because a single outlier can dramatically shift the decision boundary.

Kernel Methods.description: Functions used in support vector machines to compute relationships between observations in higher-dimensional spaces without explicitly transforming the data, a technique known as the kernel trick. Common kernels include the polynomial kernel and the radial basis function (RBF) kernel.

Bias-Variance Tradeoff.description: The tension between a model's sensitivity to the training data (variance) and its systematic error (bias). Allowing misclassifications in a support vector classifier increases bias but reduces variance, leading to better generalization on new data.

Cross-Validation.description: A model evaluation technique that rotates which portion of the data is used as the test set, averaging results across all partitions. It is used to select hyperparameters such as the soft margin in support vector classifiers.

LangGraph.description: A library built on top of LangChain for constructing stateful, graph-based AI agent workflows, where nodes represent functions that modify a shared state dictionary and edges control the flow between them. It supports conditional edges and loops for complex agent behavior.

LangChain.description: A Python library that provides abstractions for building applications with large language models, including message types, tool binding, and LLM invocation. LangGraph is built on top of LangChain.

Agent State.description: A typed dictionary (TypedDict subclass) used in LangGraph to carry information between nodes in a graph, where each node reads from and writes to this shared state. Every node function must accept and return state.

Graph Nodes and Edges.description: The core building blocks of a LangGraph workflow: nodes are functions that transform state, and edges define the execution order between nodes. Conditional edges allow the graph to branch based on runtime decisions.

Conditional Edges.description: A LangGraph mechanism that routes execution to different nodes based on the output of a decider function, which returns a string label mapped to a target node. This enables branching and looping behavior within an agent graph.

ReAct Agent Pattern.description: A design pattern for AI agents (Reasoning and Acting) where the agent iteratively selects and calls tools, evaluates results, and decides when to stop, without requiring explicit stopping conditions to be programmed. It is implemented in LangGraph using a conditional loop between an LLM node and a tool node.

Tool Calling.description: The mechanism by which an LLM agent selects and invokes Python functions decorated with @tool, which automatically generates the JSON description needed for the LLM to understand and use the function. Tools must include clear docstrings so the LLM knows how to use them.

Agent Memory.description: The maintenance of conversation history by appending human messages and AI messages to a list that is passed to the LLM on each invocation, giving the agent the context of prior turns. Without this, LLMs have no memory of previous interactions within or across sessions.

Persistence.description: The ability of an agent to retain conversation history or state across separate sessions by saving it to an external store such as a text file or database. Without persistence, all memory is lost when the application is restarted.

Context Window Management.description: Strategies for keeping the conversation history fed to an LLM within acceptable length limits, such as truncating old messages or summarizing earlier parts of the conversation. Growing context windows increase API costs and latency.

Message Types.description: Structured classes in LangChain (HumanMessage, AIMessage, SystemMessage, ToolMessage) that represent different roles in a conversation and all inherit from BaseMessage. They are used to construct the conversation history passed to the LLM.

Annotated Type and Reducers.description: A Python typing construct used in LangGraph state definitions to attach metadata or a reducer function (such as add_messages) to a field, specifying how state updates should be applied—for example, appending to a list instead of replacing it.

Multi-Agent Systems.description: Architectures where multiple specialized AI agents collaborate to solve complex tasks, often coordinated by an orchestrator agent that decides which sub-agent to invoke next. Research on multi-agent systems explores how agents can dynamically organize and learn to improve collaboration.

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation).description: An architecture that augments an LLM's responses by retrieving relevant documents from a vector database and including them in the prompt context, enabling the model to answer questions grounded in external knowledge.

Reinforcement Learning.description: A training paradigm used after initial pre-training where human evaluators rate model outputs and those ratings are used to further train the model to produce better responses. It is used in LLM alignment and in training multi-agent orchestrators.

Streamlit.description: A Python library used to build and deploy interactive web-based GUI applications for data science and machine learning projects. It is one of the deployment targets for final projects in the course.

Docker Containerization.description: A tool for packaging applications and their dependencies into portable containers, mentioned in the course syllabus as a deployment method for end-to-end data science solutions.

Law of Large Numbers.description: A statistical principle stating that the sample average of a random variable converges to its expected value as the number of observations grows large. It is invoked to justify approximating expected values with finite samples when computing KL divergence.

Hyperplane.description: A generalization of a line (in 2D) or plane (in 3D) to higher dimensions, defined by a linear equation with no squared or higher-order terms. In support vector machines, the decision boundary is always a hyperplane in the transformed feature space.

Polynomial Kernel.description: A kernel function for support vector machines that systematically increases the dimensionality of the feature space using polynomial transformations of degree D, where D is a hyperparameter tuned via cross-validation.

Radial Basis Function Kernel.description: A kernel function for support vector machines that implicitly maps data into an infinite-dimensional space and behaves like a weighted nearest-neighbor model, where closer observations have more influence on classification.

Nearest Neighbor Classification.description: A classification approach where a new observation is assigned the label of its closest neighbors in the feature space, with weighted variants giving more influence to nearer neighbors. The radial basis function kernel in SVMs behaves analogously to a weighted nearest-neighbor model.

Multiclass Classification.description: A classification setting where the target variable can take more than two class labels, such as predicting the next word in a vocabulary of 50,000 tokens. Cross entropy is the standard loss function for multiclass classification problems.

Neural Networks.description: Computational models inspired by biological neurons that learn by adjusting weights through backpropagation to minimize a loss function such as cross entropy. LLMs like GPT are large neural networks trained to predict the next token in a sequence.

Backpropagation.description: An algorithm for training neural networks by computing gradients of the loss function with respect to model weights and updating those weights to minimize the loss. It is mentioned as the mechanism by which cross entropy loss drives weight updates.

Large Language Models.description: Very large neural network models trained on massive text corpora to predict the probability of the next token in a sequence, with vocabularies of tens of thousands of words and outputs that are probability distributions over that vocabulary. They form the core reasoning component of AI agents in the course.

Soft Margin.description: The margin in a support vector classifier that allows some observations to be misclassified or to fall within the margin boundary, making the classifier more robust to outliers. The optimal soft margin is selected using cross-validation.
